channel.title: AP News 全文 RSS（简体中文版）
channel.description: AP News 首页抓取 + 英译简

item.title: 一棵据说曾庇护传奇罗宾汉的古老橡树已经死了
item.description:
伦敦（美联社）——一棵与罗宾汉传说有关的巨大古橡树可能是被爱死的。
英国皇家鸟类保护协会周四表示，舍伍德森林中拥有 1,200 年历史的大橡树据信在今年春天没有长出叶子后已经死亡。
该保护组织表示，过去两个世纪里，诺丁汉的游客参观了这棵树粗糙的树枝和蔓延的树冠，压缩了土壤，使雨水难以到达其根部。
这片森林多年来一直受到威胁，过去曾有传言说这棵树已经死亡，但该组织证实它还活着。
皇家鸟类保护协会的霍莉·德雷克在宣布这棵树死亡的声明中说：“这棵树今年未能长出叶子，这让每个人都感到心碎。”
据说，这棵树曾庇护过罗宾汉，这位 13 世纪的传奇强盗，偷富济贫，在被诺丁汉治安官追捕时，在森林里避难。
1790 年，海曼·鲁克 (Hayman Rooke) 少校在一本关于橡树的书中提到了它，并因此而得名，这导致了第一波粉丝涌入森林。
无法说出是什么杀死了这棵树，但数百万人的足迹导致了它的倒塌，以及使用电缆和杆子支撑其巨大四肢的干预措施。带来热浪和干旱的气候变化也受到指责。
树木专家发现根系被勒死并挨饿。
“像大橡树这样的古老树木是‘英国的白犀牛保护’，但它们的衰退却远不那么明显，”林地信托基金的埃德·派恩说。“拯救它们对于我们生活的世界的健康至关重要，但大多数都在没有得到大橡树的认可或照顾的情况下悄然消失。”
除了在民间传说中的地位外，这片森林还因舍伍德橡树而闻名，这些橡树曾在 18 世纪末和 19 世纪初为海军中将霍雷肖·纳尔逊 (Horatio Nelson) 率领的皇家海军的船只提供漂浮空间，并作为伦敦圣保罗大教堂 (St. Paul’s Cathedral) 屋顶的木材而闻名。
大橡树幸免于难，自 20 世纪 70 年代以来一直受到栅栏的保护。
德雷克说：“大橡树将继续矗立在舍伍德的中心，作为游客前来参观的天然纪念碑，继续生活在罗宾汉的传奇中，并继续为死后的森林生态系统提供与生前一样多的支持。”

item.title: 赫格斯攻击北约盟国并宣布对驻欧美军进行审查
item.description:
BRUSSELS (AP) — U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth lashed out at NATO allies on Thursday as he announced a six-month Pentagon review of American forces in Europe whose outcome will depend on how fast the Europeanstake responsibilityfor their own security.
The review was yet another surprise for European allies and Canada as they learn to deal with an increasingly unpredictable ally. U.S. officials and senior military officers had promised to coordinate closely with the Europeans as America draws down.
In recent months, U.S. President Donald Trump and the Pentagon have sentconflicting signalsabout whether America is reducing or increasing its military footprint in Europe, as well asthreatening to annex Greenland, a semiautonomous island that is part of ally Denmark. Just weeks ago, the Trump administration said that it would no longer provide as much military support should any NATO member come under attack.
AP correspondent Karen Chammas reports Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has lashed out at NATO allies and announced a review of U.S. forces in Europe.
“This will be a real review. It will be designed to ensure that NATO is moving fast and irreversibly toward Europe leading, stepping up to take primary responsibility for the defense of Europe,” Hegseth told his NATO counterparts. “It’s a review that some countries will fail and others will pass with flying colors.”
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz later said the allies have long been aware of U.S. plans to pull troops from Europe at some point and that they must take care of their own security.
“We know that we must do more and we are doing it,” Merz said.
In a fiery speech at NATO headquarters in Brussels, Hegseth lambasted European allies for failing to provide U.S. forces access tobases in Europeto launch attacks on Iran, calling it “shameful.”
“These allies, they put America’s sons and daughters, our sons and daughters, at risk by denying them the predictable access, basing and overflight that never should have been in question at all,” he said. The review would also assess whether the U.S. has full access and overflight “when we need it.”
While defense ministers and military officers sat in silence, Hegseth railed against migration and gender equality policies in Europe, in remarks reminiscent to those ofVice President JD Vancein February last year that angered many Europeans.
“Instead of tanks and fighters and air defenses, the focus has been on gender equity and climate change and defense austerity. Europe’s borders flew wide open, welfare states expanded, defense budgets cratered, along with Europe’s belief in itself and its civilization,” Hegseth said.
Hegseth’s comments largely mischaracterized European policies today. On defense, European allies and Canada have launched an unprecedented effort to boost defense spending and expand their armed forces. NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte noted on Thursday that they spent $90 billion more on defense last year, a 20% increase over 2024. And while Europe accepted large numbers of migrants and asylum seekers more than a decade ago, most countries have tightened their borders since.
It does not augur well for a summit of NATO leaders in Turkey on July 7-8.
It was a rare visit to NATO by Hegseth, his first this year after skipping a meeting in February. The Pentagon chief did not stay long, leaving well before the gathering was over and hours before Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy was due topress allies for more weaponsfor his country.
Speaking to reporters at Brussels airport before flying home, Hegseth said, “It was great to hear country after country say, ‘We’re going to meet our target. We’re going to meet our target.’ There are still a few outliers, and we will be clear with them as we do this review.”
NATO’s supreme allied commander, an American, is working onbackup plansto defend Europe after the U.S. signaled on June 3 that it would no longer supply an aircraft carrier and support ships, aerial refueling planes and dozens of fighter jets, among other military assets, in a crisis.
The Trump administration insists that it needs to be able to plan for two simultaneous conflicts and wants more military resources at hand should it clash with China in the Indo-Pacific region.
Under NATO’s collective security guarantee –Article 5of its founding treaty – the 32 allies pledge that an attack on one of them will be considered an attack on all. It does not oblige them to provide military support, although many likely would.
In essence, the United States is scaling back how it might help should an ally trigger Article 5.
The U.S. has by far NATO’s biggest armed forces. It does not intend to withdraw its nuclear weapons in Europe, which are key to NATO’s deterrence. To underscore that point, NATO’s Nuclear Planning Group issued its first statement in 19 years after Thursday’s meeting.
In the statement, it “recalled that the strategic nuclear forces of the Alliance remain the supreme guarantee of Allied security and underpin NATO’s extended deterrence architecture.”
The ministers “agreed to continue enhancing NATO’s nuclear deterrence mission by modernizing NATO’s nuclear capabilities, strengthening its nuclear planning capacity, and adapting to achieve its security interests.”
Rutte played down the impact of the U.S. decision, saying that the NATO Force Model – the system for organizing what forces member countries will provide commanders in times of peace, crisis or conflict – is just “a planning tool,” and not a reflection of what would actually happen.
“If war breaks out, we will all max out what we need to do to make sure we can fight the war,” Rutte told reporters. “In the planning phase, it is important to know what we can count on. What is in theory there.”

item.title: 乌克兰无人机在对俄罗斯首都的一次重大袭击中点燃了莫斯科一家炼油厂
item.description:
Ukraine struck a major Moscow oil refinery Thursday for a second time in a week, sending huge plumes of black smoke over the capital and disrupting hundreds of flights at its airports in one of its biggest drone attacks since Russia’sfull-scale invasionover four years ago, officials said.
Ukraine hasrepeatedly targeted Russian oil facilities, aiming to cut Moscow’s revenue for the war and make Russians feel the consequences of the invasion. Some areas have reportedfuel shortages.
The attack by dozens of drones came hours after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said he had held “an important coordination call” with the presidents of the United States and France and had wonkey pledges of further supportfrom this week’s G7 summit.
“If Ukraine is going to burn, your Moscow will burn too,” Zelenskyy said, adding that the attack was part of Kyiv’s effort to bring Russian President Vladimir Putin to the negotiating table. “It is time to end the aggression, time to end this war.”
The Moscow attack was the latest embarrassment for Putin. Ukrainian drones attackedhis hometown of St. Petersburgearlier this month as he welcomed foreign VIPs to his showcase economic forum in the city.
Putin on Thursday was in Kazan, some 700 kilometers (430 miles) east of Moscow, hosting leaders of theAssociation of Southeast Asian Nationsas Russia seeks to bolster business and other ties with the regional bloc.
Russia’s state-controlled TV channels only briefly mentioned the attack on Moscow. Pro-Kremlin newspapers reported it, with some praising the performance of air defenses while noting that the strike highlighted the need to further strengthen the defensive shield around the capital.
Vyacheslav Volodin, speaker of the lower house of Russia’s parliament, warned that Moscow would respond by ramping up its strikes.
“Their action will lead to our counteraction and launching harsher blows, with more powerful weapons,” Volodin said in televised remarks.
Some Russian hawks urged the Kremlin to respond with nuclear weapons. Nationalist Konstantin Malofeyev criticized the military for “fighting at half-strength in a gentlemanlike way.”
“War means victory at any cost,” Malofeyev wrote on his Telegram channel, suggesting the use of “the nuclear weapons that our ancestors created and stockpiled while mobilizing the entire country’s strength precisely for this purpose -– to win.”
Thick, black smoke and occasional flames spewed from the Moscow Oil Refinery amid its red-and-white smokestacks on the southeastern edge of the city, about 15 kilometers (9 miles) from the Kremlin. Sooty, black rain fell on cars, according to local video.
“One of the most popular questions asked by Muscovites this morning is ‘What is going on?’” Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha said in a post on X. “I can answer. Your country started a war of aggression against ours. For years, it has been killing our people. Now that you know what’s going on, ask Putin when he is planning to end it.”
The refinery is one of Russia’s biggest, according to its website, producing more than a third of the Moscow region’s fuel. It was last attacked by Ukraine on Tuesday, but officials said that fire was swiftly put out.
Thursday’s fire at the refinery was “largely contained,” Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said hours afterward, adding that remaining hotspots were being extinguished.
As Ukraine pressed its strikes on Russia’s energy infrastructure, fuel supplies appeared to be under strain. Gas station chains in multiple regions have introduced restrictions on what drivers could buy. Russian independent news outlet Agentstvo reported that one out of every four gas stations has introduced some kind of restrictions.
Authorities in the capital said in a statement hours after the attack that “supplies of oil products to Moscow and the work of all gas stations in the city continue as normal.”
The attack also temporarily halted flights from four Moscow airports, transport and aviation authorities said. The Russian business daily Kommersant counted more than 500 delayed or canceled flights at the airports, based on their online flight information.
In the greater Moscow region, a drone hit a residential building in the town of Zhukovsky, according to Gov. Andrei Vorobyov. Buildings elsewhere were damaged by drone debris, injuring 17 people, including two children, he added.
“Russia is on the back foot: militarily, economically and politically,” ‪EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas‬ said on X after meeting Thursday with Ukrainian Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov. “Now is the time to provide Ukraine with even greater support and to exert even more pressure on Russia to end the war.”
Zelenskyy held talks Thursday in Brussels with NATO and European Union leaders, and the German and Ukrainian defense ministers signed an agreement to jointly develop an air defense system to counter ballistic missiles. Zelenskyy described it as the start of an “anti-ballistic missile coalition” and invited others to join.
Russia hasrelentlessly struck Ukrainewith those types of missiles, which air defenses struggle to counter.
The Russian Defense Ministry said its air defenses overnight shot down 555 Ukrainian drones over multiple regions, with almost 200 intercepted as they approached Moscow. That was roughly double the number of drones that Russia launched at Ukraine overnight, according to the Ukrainian air force.
“If Putin does not want to end this war and wants to continue it, we will not sit quietly — we will respond,” Zelenskyy said in a voice message to a group chat with journalists.
He has accepted an unconditional ceasefire demanded by U.S. President Donald Trump, but Putin has refused, and U.S.-led peace efforts have fizzled.
Along with pledges of more diplomatic and military help at the G7 summit, Ukraine recently has gained momentum on the battlefield against Russia’s bigger army, thanks to its high-tech drones, Western officials and analysts say. Longer-range drones are choking Russian supply lines in occupied regions of Ukraine, in addition to disrupting oil production.
French President Emmanuel Macronsaid the G7 summit was “very important for Ukraine” because its supporters — crucially including the U.S. — vowed to help it, although he provided no details. The U.S. under Trump has cut back assistance to Ukraine, leaving the Europeans as the biggest suppliers of military and financial aid. Trump and Zelenskyy have had a sometimes strained relationship.
“America is with us on Ukraine, that is very important,” Macron said as he and Trump left the Palace of Versailles near Paris.
In other developments Thursday, Russia struck the city of Sumy in northeastern Ukraine with two glide bombs that killed a 64-year-old man who was fishing in a river, said Oleh Hryhorov, head of the regional military administration. A Russian strike on the central city of Dnipro killed one man and wounded nine, said Oleksandr Hanzha, head of the Dnipropetrovsk regional military administration.
Follow the AP’s coverage of the war in Ukraine athttps://apnews.com/hub/russia-ukraine

item.title: 德克萨斯州空难中，志愿者们拿着大锤和铲子竞相营救乘客
item.description:
Thebusiness jet barreledcockeyed down the dark highway, knocking down one light pole after another, an orange glow of sparks trailing it.
From a distance, Ivan Franco thought it must be a car. But as he approached in his tow truck, he saw it was a plane — broken in half, its fuselage resting on its side, bright fire beginning to rise above. He stopped and rifled through the rescue kit his company keeps in the truck, grabbing a sledgehammer as well as three fire extinguishers, which he handed off to police officers.
“At that moment, you don’t think much about what to do, because I knew the plane could explode since it was on fire,” Franco told The Associated Press in Spanish. “My idea was to try to break the windows because the pilots hadn’t come out yet.”
Franco was one of several motorists who happened across the crash in Laredo, Texas, late Tuesday night and rushed to help — putting their own lives in danger to help those on board escape as smoke filled the cabin.
Police were also on the scene quickly, and their teamwork with the good Samaritans undoubtedly saved lives, officials said.
“The officers and the good Samaritans that went to the scene, our firefighters that responded — I do also want to commend each and every one of them,” Laredo Police Chief Mike Rodriguez said during a news conference Wednesday. He said he asked his staff to track down all the civilians who helped.
The Cessna Citation Latitude twin jet departed Tuesday evening from the Mexican resort city of San José del Cabo and was bound for Austin, Texas, the FAA said in a statement. The plane was operated by NetJets, a company owned by Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway that lets people buy part ownership in private jets. NetJets said in a statement that it was cooperating with authorities.
The crash occurred after its pilots reported mechanical problems while requesting an emergency landing at a nearby airport. The fuselage came to rest across a concrete barrier, while its tail broke off and fell to a lower section of roadway.
One person was killed:Joshua Baer,a leader in Texas’ technology and startup sectors. Three teenage passengers and two pilots survived, as did a person in a truck struck by the plane as it crashed. Authorities have not released more detail about the passengers’ connections to one another.
Investigators combed through wreckage Wednesday for clues to the cause.
It was thethird significant aviation accidentin as many days in the U.S. AB-52 crashedMonday during a test flight at Edwards Air Force Base in California and killed all eight people aboard, while on Sunday,12 people were killedwhen a plane on a skydiving outing in Missouri crashed.
Among the motorists who stopped in Laredo was Zayra Garza, an esthetician who was driving her co-workers home when she saw the wreckage. She recorded video as her husband ran to help.
“It looked like part of a movie. I was in shock,” Garza said. Most worrisome was the fire: “I was concerned that it could have just exploded at any time.”
Garza saw people leave their cars to try to smash the cockpit glass. Her video shows the aircraft’s door popping open slightly from inside as a voice cries “Help! Help! Help!” The rescuers strain to lift the door farther open as the three teenagers dart out, followed quickly by one pilot and then by another.
Franco, a 23-year-old from Laredo, frantically swung the sledgehammer through heavy smoke. Others struck at the window with a shovel and tools from their own vehicles.
They accomplished little more than spiderwebbing the cockpit window with small cracks: Airplane windshields have multiple layers of glass and are designed to remain structurally sound even if the outer layer shatters. The windows must be able to withstand a bird strike at cruising speed and hold up to extreme pressure differences at high altitudes.
“They are basically bulletproof,” said retired airline pilot John Cox, who is CEO of Safety Operating Systems.
Police officers tried to remove the final person inside — Baer — as the smoke grew thicker. Officers doubled over coughing after turning away from the smoke.
Eventually firefighters with oxygen masks were able to get inside.
Firefighters also removed a dog from the plane that was suffering from smoke inhalation. The dog was turned over to animal control and was expected to survive, said Jose Baeza, an investigator with the Laredo Police Department.
Five officers were treated for smoke inhalation; the five people who survived the crash were also released from a hospital.
As the plane crashed on the northbound lanes of the highway, its wing hit a truck traveling southbound. The driver of that vehicle also survived, Baeza said.
There has been an outpouring of support on social media for those who stopped to help, heralding their bravery and selflessness.
Laredo Mayor Victor Treviño called it “nothing short of a miracle that this tragedy did not become a mass fatality event,” thanks in part to the late hour when the crash occurred and the quick action of first responders.
Franco said that as he tried to help, all he could think of was getting people out of the plane. But to do it, he had to conquer another feeling.
“You’re in constant fear,” he said. “You don’t know what situation you’re in.”
Johnson and Golden reported from Seattle. Taxin reported from Santa Ana, California. AP journalists Josh Funk in Omaha, Nebraska; Dave Collins in Hartford, Connecticut; Jeffrey Collins in Columbia, South Carolina; and Russ Bynum in Savannah, Georgia, contributed.

item.title: 纽约中央公园青少年从马车上摔下身亡
item.description:
纽约（美联社）——据警方称，周三，中央公园的一匹马车从车夫身边逃走，一名青少年被抛倒在地，导致一名青少年死亡。
据纽约警察局称，事故发生在下午 3 点左右，当时这名 18 岁的男子与另外三名乘客乘坐马车。至少有两名乘客从倾斜的驾驶室中飞出。
这名少年最初被送往医院，情况危急。其他乘客拒绝接受治疗。
代表运输行业员工的运输工人工会的一名代表表示，司机下车给乘客拍照，他们不应该这样做。
工会当地分会行政副主席亚历山大·坎普 (Alexander Kemp) 表示，这匹马在公园里只呆了六周。他说他希望进行全面调查。
他在一份声明中表示：“公园的安全问题越来越受到人们的关注，所有车辆都需要进行改进，包括电动自行车、送货车、三轮车和马车。”
视频显示，这匹马在公园里奔跑，同时有两个人从四轮马车上跳下来。第二段视频显示，在公园繁忙的环线上，出租车撞到另一辆马车的车轮后翻倒。
对于中央公园拥有 150 年历史的马车行业来说，这是一个令人担忧的时刻。长期以来，该行业一直被视为一个古色古香的景点，它为游客提供了昔日纽约的浪漫遗迹，同时为司机以及许多农场和赛马提供了数百个工作岗位。但他们现在面临着来自反对者越来越大的禁令威胁，他们称这些游乐设施不仅对马匹不人道，而且对城市居民构成危险。
周三的活动是在公园最近发生的几起与马有关的问题之后发生的，其中包括上周一匹马摔倒致死的事件。
中央公园保护协会（Central Park Conservancy）是管理该公园的非营利组织，去年夏天曾公开支持禁止马车，该组织表示，连续发生的事件应该会结束该行业。
该组织在一份声明中表示：“一名年轻人来到我们的公园游玩，结果失去了生命。”“对于在美国使用最频繁的公共空间之一运营的过时行业来说，这种成本是不可接受的。”
中央公园占地近 850 英亩，每年吸引数百万人前来。

item.title: 最高法院支持一名德克萨斯州男子，他表示吸食大麻的人拥有枪支并不构成犯罪
item.description:
华盛顿（美联社）——最高法院周四裁定反对一项广泛的联邦禁止大麻使用者拥有枪支的禁令，这是法院扩大枪支权利的一系列枪支案件中的最新一起。
法官们一致做出了对德克萨斯州男子阿里·丹尼尔·赫马尼有利的判决，他认为禁止经常使用非法毒品的人持有枪支的法律违反了第二修正案。
2026 年 4 月 23 日，加利福尼亚州圣安娜的 Tropicanna 药房和杂草配送中心展示出售的大麻克隆。（美联社照片/Jae C. Hong）
尼尔·戈萨奇法官写道，他的观点严格限制了政府从不被认为危险的吸毒者手中夺取枪支的权力。赫马尼没有被指控犯有任何其他罪行，也没有被指控在酒后使用武器，他很庆幸自己“终于结案了”，律师扎卡里·纽兰说。
这一决定对唐纳德·特朗普总统的共和党政府来说是一个损失，尽管该政府反对其他枪支限制，但仍捍卫了 1968 年的法律。戈萨奇写道，其核心论点“无论从哪方面衡量都失败了”。
戈萨奇写道，该法律最初的目的是让枪支远离危险的人，但现在有数百万人使用大麻。它在大约一半的州广泛合法，并已广泛用于健康目的。
戈萨奇写道：“无论人们如何看待这些事态发展，联邦政府不仅容忍了它们，而且还助长了它们。”“所有这些都让它处于尴尬的境地，表明现在经常使用大麻的数百万美国人绝对是异常危险的。”
该法律还被用于针对亨特·拜登的案件中，亨特·拜登因 2018 年吸食可卡因而购买枪支而在特拉华州威尔明顿被定罪。他后来被他的父亲、民主党总统乔·拜登赦免。
亨特·拜登于 2024 年 6 月 10 日离开位于特拉华州威尔明顿的联邦法院。（美联社照片/马特·洛克）
在周四的决定之后，吸毒成瘾的人仍有可能受到起诉。
戈萨奇写道：“我们不会讨论禁止吸毒者或目前喝醉的人拥有枪支的努力。”他说，如果检察官有证据证明吸食大麻的人是危险的，他们可以对吸食大麻的人提出指控。
即使特朗普政府四月份将医用大麻重新归类为危险性较低的药物，娱乐性使用在联邦层面仍然是非法的。
这个案例催生了一些不寻常的政治联盟。
美国公民自由联盟和全国步枪协会以及 NORML 等大麻合法化组织都支持 Hemani 的案件。另一方是包括Everytown在内的枪支安全组织，他们通常在第二修正案问题上反对政府。
美国公民自由联盟对这一裁决表示赞赏，称近一半的美国人曾在生命中的某个时刻吸食过大麻。
美国公民自由联盟法律总监塞西莉亚·王(Cecillia Wang)表示：“法院发出了一个强烈的信息，即政府不能通过对大量人员是否危险做出明确且毫无根据的假设来将他们的行为定为犯罪。”
第二修正案基金会称其为“枪支拥有者的重大胜利”。
反对大麻合法化的 Smart Approaches to Marijuana 组织谴责了法院的裁决。
首席执行官凯文·萨贝特 (Kevin Sabet) 表示：“虽然本案中的法官似乎最关心的是有关第二修正案权利的历史斗争，但公共健康和安全是这一决定的附带损害。”
枪支管制组织则更加沉默，Everytown 表示，该决定仍然承认“毒品和枪支可以形成危险的混合体”。
很少看到针对仅被指控拥有枪支和吸毒的人提出单独的刑事指控。这项指控更常针对还被指控犯有其他罪行的人。
该意见是最高法院提交的一系列枪支案件中的最新一起，该裁决在 2022 年扩大枪支权利的里程碑式裁决在全国范围内引发了一波挑战。
此后，高等法院维持了一项旨在保护家庭暴力受害者的法律，并对幽灵枪械进行了严格规定，但取消了对撞火枪托（一种能够快速射击的配件）的禁令。法官们还在考虑本学期的第二起枪支案件，原因是夏威夷对携带枪支的严格规定。
2025 年 10 月 15 日，纽约市警察局提供的幽灵枪陈列在曼哈顿地区检察官办公室。（美联社照片/Richard Drew）
在这张 2017 年 10 月 4 日的资料照片中，犹他州南乔丹的 Gun Vault 商店和射击场的半自动步枪上安装了一种名为撞火枪托的装置。（美联社照片/里克·鲍默，档案）
请关注美联社对美国最高法院的报道：https://apnews.com/hub/us-supreme-court。

item.title: 受特朗普支持的俄克拉荷马州牧师因不当短信报道退出共和党众议院决选
item.description:
俄克拉荷马州大型教会牧师杰克逊·拉迈耶 (Jackson Lahmeyer) 退出了俄克拉荷马州美国众议院席位的决选，此前有报道称，他向一名不是他妻子的女性发送了浪漫的短信。
进入八月决选的第二天，拉迈耶周三发表声明称，“在过去二十四小时内与我的妻子肯德拉和我的团队虔诚地考虑之后”，他做出了暂停竞选活动的“艰难决定”。
拉迈耶补充道：“我不想分散我的家人、教会和俄克拉荷马州第一国会选区伟大人民的注意力，他们应该在华盛顿得到代表他们的强有力的保守派声音。”
就在唐纳德·特朗普总统支持他的决选对手马克·特德福德几分钟后，拉迈耶宣布退出的电子邮件声明就出现在收件箱中。
特朗普写道：“我非常感谢杰克逊·拉迈耶在困难的情况下所做的辛勤工作。”他接着致电俄克拉荷马州代表特德福德，“一路支持特朗普和MAGA！”
周一，即俄克拉荷马州初选前一天，特朗普重申了对拉迈耶的支持，他上个月最初支持拉迈耶，并赞扬他为特朗普创立了牧师。全国信仰领袖联盟是致力于支持特朗普 2024 年竞选的团体之一。
《每日邮报》周日报道称，拉迈耶与一名为他的竞选活动筹款的女性交换了数千条浪漫短信。多家新闻机构报道称，拉迈耶在一篇现已删除的社交媒体帖子中承认了自己的行为，称此事“已经私下处理”，并称自己“通过短信跨越了界限”。
拉迈耶的竞选团队没有立即回复信息，询问他在结束竞选活动之前是否与特朗普交谈过，或者为什么他删除了自己的社交媒体账户。
在拉迈耶所牧养的塔尔萨会众谢里登教会的网站上，他被列为“余民崛起”活动阵容中的一员。其他发言者包括特朗普的前国家安全顾问迈克尔·弗林将军，他在共和党第一个任期内承认在与俄罗斯高级外交官的谈话中向联邦调查局撒谎，后来被赦免。
可以通过 http://x.com/MegKinnardAP 联系 Meg Kinnard

item.title: 美国职棒大联盟警告球员改变骄傲之夜上限，共和党人注意到了。这是你需要知道的
item.description:
美国职棒大联盟球队在骄傲月期间为促进 LGBTQ+ 包容性所做的努力并没有得到一些普通球员的热烈欢迎，而联盟的回应也引发了共和党知名政客的批评。
旧金山巨人队的几名成员在 6 月 12 日举行的骄傲之夜期间选择根本不戴主题帽子。几天前的晚上，洛杉矶道奇队的两名球员没有与队友一起戴彩虹主题的帽子。
美国职业棒球大联盟警告巨人队球员，在帽子上写字违反了联盟政策，尽管联盟补充说，问题不在于相应信息的内容，而在于修改帽子本身的行为。
其后果包括来自著名政界人士的批评，包括副总统 JD 万斯和密苏里州参议员乔什·霍利。
以下是有关 MLB 和骄傲之夜的信息：
在旧金山一年一度的骄傲之夜，旧金山巨人队主场 1-5 输给芝加哥小熊队的比赛中，几位旧金山巨人队的投手——首发兰登·鲁普、中继投手 JT·布鲁贝克和瑞安·沃克——在自己的帽子上加上了圣经经文。
鲁普是北卡罗来纳州人，他在黑色帽子的正面用银色记号笔写下了“Gen 9:12-16”，球队经典的“SF”标志充满了彩虹的颜色，长期以来一直是 LGBTQ+ 群体的象征。
根据《圣经新国际版》的翻译，《创世记》的段落包括：“我已将彩虹放在云中，它将成为我与大地立约的记号”。
“这只是关于上帝的圣约和他对我们做出的承诺，以及他的信实和怜悯，”鲁普后来告诉记者。“这就是我所相信的东西，我对此坚定不移，值得庆幸的是，我们生活在一个你知道的国家，我们可以自由地相信我们想要的东西，是的，并表达我们想要的东西。”
巨人队的中继投手萨姆·亨特格斯完全没有戴骄傲主题的帽子，而是选择了俱乐部常规的黑色版本，并带有橙色的“SF”字样。
联盟发表了两份声明作为回应。
第一个指出，在帽子上写字违反了联盟的规则，并警告球员未来的违规行为。
联盟周二发表另一份声明，澄清“例行口头警告”与各自信息的内容无关，而是修改球衣的行为。
联盟表示：“我们过去多次向球员发出过同样的警告，要求他们发出‘爸爸’、‘母亲节快乐，我爱妈妈’以及家庭成员的名字等信息。”
万斯引用推文提到美国职业棒球大联盟的警告，写道：“特朗普赢了，我们不必再这样做了。”
唐纳德·特朗普总统的第二届政府对 LBGTQ+ 群体的成员，特别是跨性别者群体，采取了激进的行动。
霍利周二给美国职业棒球大联盟(MLB)专员罗布·曼弗雷德(Rob Manfred)写了一封信，在信中他对向球员发出的警告表示“严重关切”。霍利称这一警告“可疑”，因为他认为美国职业棒球大联盟已经通过推出以骄傲为主题的制服来宣扬政治观点。
霍利要求美国职业棒球大联盟回应多项询问，包括过去五年中所有统一违规罚款的清单。
旧金山队一年级主教练托尼·维泰洛(Tony Vitello)的球队周三以全国联盟第二差的战绩进入球队，他正试图避免卷入这场争论。
“我不了解副总统，它不存在于我的世界里，”他说。“我的世界是，‘我们如何找到击败勇士队的方法？’然后，如果我们能击败他们，就尝试再次击败他们。所以希望我们的球员专注于他们必须做的事情，并以正确的方式处理他们的业务。”
2022 年，坦帕湾光芒队的少数球员在球队的骄傲之夜期间拒绝佩戴彩虹色徽标。现在是圣地亚哥教士队成员的中继员贾森·亚当（Jason Adam）称这是一个“基于信仰”的决定。
亚当在 2022 年对《坦帕湾时报》表示：“这正是我们所相信的生活方式，他（耶稣）鼓励我们为自己的利益而活，而不是克制。但我们爱这些男人和女人，我们关心他们，我们希望他们在这里感到安全和受欢迎。”
MLB 在北美四大职业体育联盟中的独特之处在于，其常规赛的一部分时间在六月，这在许多国家（包括美国）被视为骄傲月。除了一支 MLB 球队（德克萨斯游骑兵队）外，所有 MLB 球队都在 6 月安排了骄傲之夜。
其他联盟，包括 NBA 和 NHL，确实有大多数球队在赛季期间举办骄傲主题之夜。NHL 在 2022-23 赛季期间围绕“骄傲之夜”发生了一系列备受瞩目的争吵。
该赛季，几名球员选择不穿以骄傲为主题的赛前球衣，至少有两支球队——纽约游骑兵队和明尼苏达狂野队——在最初宣传会举办骄傲之夜后拒绝举办。两个团队都没有给出该决定背后的原因。

item.title: 辛辛那提大学对代理人声称学校了解布伦丹·索斯比赌博的说法提出异议
item.description:
辛辛那提（美联社）——辛辛那提大学体育系对布伦丹·索斯比经纪人的说法提出异议，后者表示学校知道四分卫赌博的事情，但没有采取任何行动。
学校体育部门周三在一份声明中表示：“我们所有的学生运动员全年都会多次接受广泛的赌博教育，我们绝不会故意与违反 NCAA 体育博彩规定的运动员比赛。”“如果我们发现违规投注，我们会向 NCAA 报告并遵守制裁规定。”
索斯比的经纪人罗恩·斯莱文 (Ron Slavin) 在接受达拉斯 KRLD 电台采访时做出了上述说法。
NCAA 禁止索斯比参加比赛，原因包括赌博活动，包括在 2022 年印第安纳州球员名单上对他自己的球队进行投注以及对职业体育进行投注。今年，他放弃了史无前例的法律斗争，转而为德克萨斯理工大学效力。
索斯比周二宣布参加美国国家橄榄球联盟补充选秀，并必须在周一之前提交申请。他试图加快自己的职业生涯，这在很大程度上依赖于很少使用的休赛期流程，该流程是为不符合联盟初选条件的前景而设计的。
索斯比因诊断出的毒瘾而接受了一个月的住院治疗。然后他起诉了 NCAA，并获得了法庭下令的复职，这引发了全国范围内对德克萨斯理工大学的强烈反对。
一位知情人士告诉美联社，他暂定计划于 7 月 10 日在达拉斯地区的一所高中参加 NFL 球队的训练。该人士不愿透露姓名，因为补充草案的实施过程尚未完成。
辛辛那提大学正在起诉索斯比，指控他在转学到德克萨斯理工大学后违反了自己的姓名、形象和肖像合同。该大学于二月份向美国俄亥俄州南区地方法院提起诉讼。
斯莱文表示，对他的客户采取法律行动是错误的，索斯比打算对抗诉讼并赔偿由此造成的任何损失。
索斯比上赛季为熊猫队传球 2,800 码、27 次达阵和 5 次拦截，同时还跑出 580 码和 9 次得分。
美联社职业橄榄球作家斯凯勒·迪克森为本报告做出了贡献。

item.title: 奥运奖牌得主珍妮·辛普森在参加英里组比赛时因身体不适而倒下
item.description:
北卡罗来纳州罗利（美联社）——周二晚上，奥运会铜牌得主、1500 米赛跑世界冠军珍妮·辛普森 (Jenny Simpson) 在罗利举行的一场比赛中，在英里组配速时突发医疗事故，不幸倒地。
辛普森在赛场接受了心肺复苏术，并在将她送往医院之前使用了自动体外除颤器。跑跑网最先报告了这一医疗紧急情况。
39 岁的辛普森正在参加沃尔特跑步爵士主办的社区田径活动，鼓励各个级别的跑步者参加。这次活动让我们有机会见到辛普森。
周三，“沃尔特爵士跑步团队”在 Instagram 上写道：“我们非常感谢立即做出反应的个人，以及以如此谨慎、紧迫和专业的态度处理这一情况的 EMS 和医疗专业人员。”
声明还说：“珍妮正在接受优质的医疗护理，这段时间我们的心与她和她的家人同在。”
辛普森在2016年里约热内卢奥运会上获得1500米铜牌。她还在2011年世锦赛上赢得了1500米的冠军，并在2013年和2017年获得银牌。

item.title: 萨布丽娜·卡彭特 (Sabrina Carpenter) 因试图闯入她家的男子而被判处 5 年限制令
item.description:
洛杉矶（美联社）——一名男子十几次试图闯入流行歌星萨布丽娜·卡彭特的家，周三，一名法官被法官下令五年内不得接近她。
31 岁的威廉·阿普尔盖特 (William Applegate) 在一次听证会上表示，他和卡彭特是一项机密军事计划的一部分，该计划要求他们“尽快聚在一起”，因为这对“国家和全球安全”至关重要。
阿普尔盖特在一份请愿书中说，上个月，她在到达洛杉矶卡彭特家的前门时袭击了一名保安并被捕。他是通过邻近的一处房产到达那里的。随后几天他回来了，法官于 5 月 29 日发布了临时限制令。
阿普尔盖特承认了他所有的出现，并说卡彭特希望他在那里。然而，他表示，如果她亲自告诉他，他“非常愿意”远离她。他说警方和她的代表正在与他作对。
在没有律师的情况下，他穿着西装，拿着笔记本电脑坐在辩护桌旁，连贯地传达了信息。
卡彭特准备远程作证，但没有被传唤这样做。
她的律师布莱尔·伯克告诉法官“她担心自己和家人的安全。”伯克询问阿普尔盖特只是为了核实有关卡彭特的社交媒体帖子是否来自他。
阿普尔盖特在反对限制令的文件中表示，他四月份参加了卡彭特的科切拉音乐节表演，她在唱歌时看着他，试图与他交流。
卡彭特在请愿书中称他是“一个完全陌生的人”，她从未见过面或与之交流过，也不想这样做。
大卫·沃瑟曼法官在质询中认真对待了阿普尔盖特的主张，并表示他对法庭上每个人的礼貌表示赞赏。
“我理解你的信念，为了拯救世界，你和请愿者必须在一起，”法官说。“我希望你服从命令，而不是你认为正确的命令，也不是你认为军队命令你的命令。”
他命令阿普尔盖特与卡彭特以及她的姐姐和与她同住的姐姐的伴侣保持至少 100 码（米）的距离，此外还有许多其他限制。阿普尔盖特还被命令不得试图以任何方式与她交流，也不得拥有任何枪支。
阿普尔盖特仍在接受刑事调查，但法庭记录并未显示已提出指控。
27 岁的卡彭特青少年时期曾主演迪士尼频道系列剧《女孩遇见世界》，之后转向音乐事业。她的前几张录音室专辑取得了一定的成功，然后在 2022 年的《Emails I Can’t Send》中取得了突破。凭借 2024 年的专辑《Short n’ Sweet》及其排名第一的单曲《Espresso》和《Please Please Please》，她成为了多项格莱美奖得主和世界上最伟大的流行歌星之一。

item.title: 电影评论：《罗宾汉之死》吸干了英国古老传奇的血液和生命
item.description:
在迈克尔·萨诺斯基的《罗宾汉之死》的开头，休·杰克曼饰演的罗宾躲在寒冷荒凉的泥炭地上。一名年轻的袭击者（杰德·克鲁特饰）从篝火外的黑暗空虚中出现。他抓住她，告诉她洗澡是个错误。他能在顺风处闻到她的味道。然后他用刀刺穿了她的头骨。
噢-德拉利，噢-德拉利，天哪，真是美好的一天。
无论您最喜欢哪个版本的罗宾汉（为 1973 年的迪士尼动画版欢呼三声），故事情节在《罗宾汉之死》中都受到了打击。没有穿着闪亮盔甲的骑士。没有快乐的人。绝对没有虚张声势。
尼古拉斯·凯奇出色的惊悚片《猪》和科幻续集《寂静之地：第一天》的导演萨诺斯基已经削弱了这位民间英雄的勇气。这是对神话的深思熟虑的反转，对讲故事的本质提出了一些令人信服的想法。但这完全是一个艰难的过程。
“罗宾汉之死”耗尽了英国古老传说的血液和生命。因此，忘记从富人那里抢劫和从穷人那里偷窃吧。这个罗宾是一个头发花白的掠夺者，他甚至不记得自己杀了多少人。至少可以说，我们距离穿紧身衣的男人非常非常远。
这是萨诺斯基电影的一个目的，就像罗伯特·艾格斯的《北方人》和大卫·洛厄里的《绿衣骑士》一样，为古老的传说带来了原始的现实主义。尽管我们可能会想到埃罗尔·弗林或凯文·科斯特纳，但罗宾汉故事的起源并不那么令人愉快。
罗宾汉最初是一个可以追溯到 12 世纪的口头故事。几百年后，第一个书面记录是民谣。萨诺斯基的电影的片名取自其中一首民谣，其中罗宾汉——早在女仆玛丽安出现之前——只是一个自耕农。随着几个世纪的流逝，罗宾汉才逐渐获得了洛克斯利罗宾爵士的服饰。
也许可以根据这些早期的、支离破碎的起源制作出一部引人入胜的电影。但《罗宾汉之死》却花费了太多的精力来磨蹭我们的脸。电影的前半部分充满了泥巴，它如此强烈地坚持其修正主义方法，以至于它很快就变得令人厌烦。
杰克曼饱经风霜，留着胡须，看起来很神奇，就像中世纪的圣诞老人。这部电影也是如此，由电影摄影师帕特里克·斯科拉 (Patrick Scola) 在崎岖、风吹的北爱尔兰远景中拍摄。罗宾本人似乎也被周围的神话所困扰。他没有使用这个名字，并称有关他的谣言是“谎言之上的谎言”。但其他人却受到鼓舞。
小约翰（比尔·斯卡斯加德饰）并不是罗宾的好朋友，而是一个不情愿的伴侣。在小约翰为了面包而打死一个人之前不久，他听小约翰谈论一个新计划，称其为“一次美好的冒险”。接下来的战斗——一场泥泞而卑鄙的混乱——更加可怕，部分原因是它完全毫无意义。
当罗宾秘密地在一座岛屿修道院避难时，现实与故事、真相与历史之间的巨大差异呈现出新的维度，布里吉德修女（朱迪·科默饰）在那里为他治疗伤口。他正在庇护小约翰的女儿玛格丽特（费丝·德莱尼饰），但他们的秘密过去却因一名年轻人（诺亚·尤佩饰）的到来而悄然受到威胁，这名年轻人受伤、缠着绷带的脸证明了他们最近与罗宾和小约翰发生了冲突。
由于布里吉德修女和他在那里找到的平静生活，罗宾的冷酷和残忍开始消失。如果故事以前是他脖子上的枷锁，那么当罗宾考虑玛格丽特的未来时，他意识到了另一个目的。
在某种程度上，《罗宾汉之死》是一个被多次重述的故事的当代版本，适合谎言和否认历史统治的时代。但这部电影中令人压抑的阴郁和被迫的愤世嫉俗让人物感到窒息，感觉并不比梅尔·布鲁克斯 1993 年的模仿作品更现实。结果虽然令人钦佩，但几乎是滑稽地误判——就像坚持帕丁顿熊坐在谎言的王座上一样。最后，《罗宾汉之死》讽刺地支持了一条老电影公理：打印传奇。
周五在 A24 影院上映的《罗宾汉之死》因强烈的血腥暴力而被美国电影协会评为 R 级。运行时间：123分钟。四颗星中的两颗星。

item.title: 西纳特拉长期担任开场演员的喜剧先驱汤姆·德雷森去世，享年 86 岁
item.description:
洛杉矶（美联社）——汤姆·德雷森 (Tom Dreesen) 于周三去世。他与搭档蒂姆·里德 (Tim Reid) 组成了美国最早的跨种族单口喜剧二人组之一，后来又在弗兰克·西纳特拉 (Frank Sinatra) 的开场表演中担任了多年。他86岁了。
据公关人员洛里·德瓦尔称，德雷森在洛杉矶的家中去世。没有提供死因。
德雷森和黑人里德在芝加哥相识后，于1969年组成了“蒂姆和汤姆”。在种族紧张局势一触即发的背景下，他们用幽默来解决社会问题，促进不同背景观众之间的理解。他们一起工作直到 20 世纪 70 年代中期。随后，里德在热门电视情景喜剧“辛辛那提的 WKRP”中以 DJ Venus Flytrap 的身份单独表演，取得了成功，德雷森客串了该剧。
“当我还是个孩子的时候，我发现了一张他和他的喜剧搭档制作的专辑，名叫《蒂姆和汤姆》，我把它带回家播放，这是一张改变了我人生轨迹的专辑。太棒了，”喜剧演员兼电影制片人迈克·宾德 (Mike Binder) 在 X 上写道。
与里德分手后，德雷森磨练了个人喜剧表演，在全国电视节目中露面超过 500 次，其中包括 60 次参加“约翰尼·卡森今夜秀”。他还是“大卫·莱特曼深夜秀”的常客和客座主持人。他们的友谊可以追溯到 20 世纪 70 年代初，当时两人都在加利福尼亚州西好莱坞的 The Comedy Store 工作。
德雷森的最后一次电视露面是上周在“拜伦·艾伦的漫画释放”节目中，该节目取代了斯蒂芬·科尔伯特取消的哥伦比亚广播公司深夜节目。
德雷森 (Dreesen) 担任西纳特拉 (Sinatra) 14 年的开场表演，并与这位艺人关系密切。
“如果他爱你，他就会崇拜你走过的土地，”德雷森在 2014 年告诉《沙漠太阳报》。“在很多方面，他就像我的父亲。我没有一个父亲真正关心我在哪里和我做了什么。但弗兰克会给我建议和忠告，然后他在很多方面都是我的朋友。我认为他很重要。”
Dressen 还与 Sinatra 的 Rat Pack 成员 Sammy Davis Jr.、Liza Minnelli、Smokey Robinson、Gladys Knight 和 Tony Orlando 一起巡演。
“他是有史以来最杰出的喜剧演员之一。今晚，他将再次为迪恩、弗兰克和萨米开场，”迪恩·马丁的女儿迪安娜·马丁在 X 上发帖称。
2008 年，他与人合着了《蒂姆和汤姆：黑白美国喜剧》一书，并于 2020 年撰写了回忆录。
德雷森出演过《科伦坡》、《她写的谋杀案》和《天使感动》等电视节目。他的电影角色包括《Spaceballs》、《Man on the Moon》和《Trouble With the Curve》，以及 HBO 电影《The Rat Pack》和《Lansky》。
德雷森积极参与慈善工作、励志演讲和退伍军人事业，包括担任加里·西尼斯基金会大使。
“美国失去了一位伟大的喜剧演员和爱国者，我也失去了一位亲爱的朋友，”西尼斯在 X 上发帖称。
他于 1939 年 9 月 11 日出生于芝加哥，在哈维郊区长大，是八个孩子之一。他 17 岁时加入美国海军，1960 年退伍后，他回到家乡从事了一系列工作，包括销售保险。
德雷森 (Dreesen) 与玛丽莲·苏博克 (Maryellen Subock) 的婚姻于 1984 年以离婚告终，留下了女儿艾米 (Amy) 和詹妮弗 (Jennifer)，以及七个孙子。他的儿子汤米先于他去世。

item.title: 渴望工作的青少年夏季求职却令人沮丧
item.description:
NEW YORK (AP) — Jaelyn Chester will wait your tables or stock your shelves. She’ll wash your dishes or scrub your toilets. If only someone would give the 17-year-old a chance.
“I’ve been looking everywhere,” says Chester, an A+ student, high school basketball star and aspiring engineer who has blanketed her community with dozens of applications. “I’m not unemployed because I’m incompetent. I’m unemployed because nobody’s hiring.”
The summer job, a rite-of-passage for generations of American teenagers, isn’t so easy to come by.
About one-third of 16- to 19-year-olds in the U.S. were employed last summer, federal data show, down from a peak of about 60% in the late 1970s. Experts’ pessimistic forecasts are combining with reports from frustrated jobless young people around the country to form a seasonal outlook far from bathed in sunshine.
“The opportunities for workers at the start of the career ladder started to dry up,” says Nicole Bachaud, an economist for ZipRecruiter, saying teens are among the labor market’s “most marginalized groups.”
Without a job, Chester worries her summer will be ruined. She wonders how she’ll fill her tank with gas and what she’ll do if she wants to go to a concert. A trip to look at colleges in North Carolina with some friends would be destined to be canceled. So her hunt continues.
Chester keeps copies of her resume in her car and has a 30-second spiel memorized when she decides to pop into a restaurant or store and try to talk with a manager. She and her friends help ready one another when they set out on their job hunt, trading tips and professional-looking clothes from their closets. Positions that once sounded awful to her, like dishwashing, no longer seem so.
“At this point,” says the teen from Lake Mary, Florida, “it would be hard to say no to anything.”
Analyzing data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, the outplacement firm Challenger, Gray and Christmas found the number of jobs secured by teens fell 25% last summer from the year prior. The firm says inflation, oil prices and cautious hiring are likely to lead to even fewer jobs this year, resulting in the lowest summer hiring total for teens since the federal government began tracking it in 1948.
Teens most commonly work in food preparation and serving jobs and sales, according to BLS data. But Jaune Little, director of recruiting services at the human resources company Insperity, says some entry-level jobs have been eliminated and teens now compete with more experienced candidates for the remaining ones.
“A lot of the entry-level roles that once existed simply do not any longer,” Little says. “Those that do exist are on leaner teams that have less ability and desire to develop and train someone. In many instances, they are prioritizing more skilled workers even if they are overqualified.”
Max Stephenson began looking for a job last year after graduating from high school. Nothing turned up all summer. Once she began at the University of Arkansas-Pulaski Technical College, she got a work study position in the cafeteria, still keeping an eye out for a more permanent gig.
Now, school’s out again, and Stephenson is again jobless.
The 19-year-old from Little Rock, Arkansas, lost track of how many jobs she’s applied for, but thinks it’s somewhere between 50 and 100. She can’t help thinking it’s tougher than previous generations had it to find work paying around the minimum wage.
“I thought it would be much easier than it’s been,” Stephenson says. “Old people say, ‘Just walk in there and give them a firm handshake.’ That doesn’t work so well now.”
A 2022 report by Pew Research Center found summer employment of teens fell during the early 2000s dot-com bubble, and dropped even more during and after the Great Recession of 2007 to 2009. White teens are more likely to have a job than teens from any other racial group, Pew found.
Across demographics, though, teens are reporting difficult job searches, taking to Reddit and TikTok with rants about phantom postings, managers who ghost them and applications that go nowhere.
It’s a struggle Connor Vukelich knows well.
After he turned 16, he applied anywhere he could find in a 30-mile radius of his home near Vancouver, Washington. No offers followed and Vukelich’s friends were similarly coming up empty-handed.
“There’s all these ‘We’re Hiring’ signs but no one’s actually hiring,” Vukelich says. “What’s going on? Why can’t any of us find jobs?”
When his search turned fruitless, he ended up working on his parents’ lavender farm. But the frustration of the experience led Vukelich – who is now 20 and a student at Embry–Riddle Aeronautical University – to launch Poppin’ Jobs, an employment search site launched this year and aimed at teens and 20-somethings.
Vukelich believes artificial intelligence is robbing teens of some potential jobs and that laws to boost the minimum wage in some states have pitted first-time job-seekers against more experienced candidates.
“They don’t see the value in hiring someone without any experience,” he says of employers, “they’re not as willing to give someone that shot.”
Some teen applicants find painful job searches eventually pay off. Demie Njea, a 16-year-old from Lexington, Kentucky, started applying for jobs once she turned 14, her state’s legal working age. A search centered on fast food spots and stores turned to one that included jobs as a janitor, daycare worker and more.
Nothing went anywhere the first summer. Or the second. Njea estimates she applied for more than 100 jobs in all. She started wondering if she’d ever get a first job.
Finally, an offer came and Njea started working at Sonic. She is thrilled. But when a friend who turned 15 started applying for work, Njea had to be honest.
“I had to calmly put her down and say, ‘You’re not going to get it,’” Njea says. “It’s just not going to happen.”
Matt Sedensky can be reached at[email protected]andhttps://x.com/sedensky

item.title: 美国天然气价格自 3 月份以来首次低于 4 美元，但仍比去年高 25%
item.description:
纽约（美联社）——周四美国平均汽油价格跌至每加仑略低于 4 美元，这让那些在华盛顿与伊朗的战争中成本飙升的司机们松了口气。
但加油费用仍然比冲突开始前要高。
根据 AAA 汽车俱乐部的数据，目前一加仑普通汽油的平均价格为 3.999 美元。这是自三月份以来价格首次如此低。油价下跌与整体原油成本下降相一致，人们对美国和伊朗之间结束战争的初步协议持乐观态度。
尽管如此，美国司机每加仑支付的费用比 2 月份美国与以色列一起攻击伊朗之前要高出约 1 美元，而且价格比去年同期高出 25%。这导致许多家庭收紧其他领域的预算，或者重新考虑他们想要更广泛地花钱的地方。
天然气并不是战争期间唯一变得更加昂贵的东西。由于全球供应链中断，食品杂货、机票甚至避孕套和鞋子的价格都上涨了。
专家警告说，即使石油和化肥等其他核心必需品再次开始从中东流出，价格冲击可能会比战斗持续得更久。
雪城大学供应链实践教授帕特·彭菲尔德 (Pat Penfield) 周四表示：“预计 2026 年剩余时间，美国各地的产品价格将继续攀升。”
彭菲尔德指出，战争造成的库存枯竭和持续的供应链后果——例如，农民已经不得不在春季支付更高的化肥和其他用品成本，这将“波及到秋季粮食价格上涨”。在加油站，他指出，美国有限的炼油厂产能“仍然是降低价格的一个重大瓶颈”。
高昂的燃料成本已将美国通胀推至三年来的最高水平。许多消费者仍然以每加仑 4 美元以上的价格加注油箱。
该价格是全国平均价格，由于供应距离和税率不同等因素，各州的成本有所不同。周四，按照 AAA 标准，加利福尼亚州普通汽油的平均价格约为每加仑 5.64 美元，其次是夏威夷的 5.57 美元。与此同时，印第安纳州和德克萨斯州的价格分别约为每加仑 3.40 美元和 3.49 美元。
最近燃料价格的下跌带来了原油（汽油的主要成分）成本的下降。
国际标准布伦特原油周四跌破每桶 78 美元。美国基准原油价格跌至每桶略高于 74 美元。这仍然比战前大约 70 美元的价格稍高一些，但远低于几周前 100 多美元的价格。
在唐纳德·特朗普总统与伊朗签署协议后，价格隔夜下跌。这项脆弱的协议要求德黑兰稀释其高浓缩铀库存，并放弃美国支持的对该国的制裁——华盛顿做出重大让步，立即允许伊朗自由出售石油。
根据劳埃德名单情报公司的海事数据，自周三签署谅解备忘录以来，主要船东也开始让船只通过重要的霍尔木兹海峡，尽管一些人报告说，只有更有限的支线是开放的。美国副总统 JD 万斯周四表示，海军已经解除了自己的封锁，允许一些船只进出伊朗港口。
尽管如此，交通量可能需要几周或几个月的时间才能恢复到战前的水平。战前，该海峡输送了世界五分之一的原油。减少产量的海湾石油生产商将需要时间来让石油重新运转。
一些船长可能会花一些时间来确定通道是否安全。美国和伊朗之间的协议要求永久停止敌对行动，并开始为期 60 天的谈判，以就伊朗核计划的未来达成最终协议，尽管特朗普为恢复袭击敞开了大门。
炼油厂通常也会提前一个月或更长时间支付原油费用，因此即使油价下跌，他们也不会立即加工更便宜的产品。在更加依赖中东进口的地区，尤其是亚洲和非洲国家，能源冲击更为严重。
